Betty Clarke of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* commented that her "powerful voice is hard-edged"[[375]](#cite_note-375) while [Rob Sheffield](/wiki/Rob_Sheffield "Rob Sheffield") from *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* described Perry's vocals on *Teenage Dream* as "processed [staccato](/wiki/Staccato "Staccato") blips".[[356]](#cite_note-SheffieldDream-356) Darren Harvey of [musicOMH](/wiki/MusicOMH "MusicOMH") compared Perry's vocals on *One of the Boys* to Alanis Morissette's, both possessing a "perky voice shifting octaves mid-syllable".[[376]](#cite_note-376) Alex Miller from *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* felt that "Perry's problem is often her voice" on *One of the Boys*, stating that "somewhere along the line someone convinced her she was like, well, a ballsy rock chick".[[377]](#cite_note-377) Conversely, Bernadette McNulty from *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* praised her "rock chick voice" in a review of a concert promoting *Prism*.[[378]](#cite_note-378)

## Public image

[![Perry performing in a dress decorated with peppermint swirls](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Katy_Perry_2011.jpg/250px-Katy_Perry_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katy_Perry_2011.jpg)

Perry in her signature spinning peppermint swirl dress at the [California Dreams Tour](/wiki/California_Dreams_Tour "California Dreams Tour") (2011)

On social media, Perry surpassed [Justin Bieber](/wiki/Justin_Bieber "Justin Bieber") as the most followed person on [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") in November 2013.[[379]](#cite_note-379) She received a Guinness record for most Twitter followers,[[380]](#cite_note-Guinness2015-380) and became the first person to gain 100 million followers on the site in June 2017.[[381]](#cite_note-381) Keith Caulfield of *Billboard* stated that Perry is "the rare celebrity who seems to have enormous popularity but genuine ground-level interaction with her adoring KatyCats".[[382]](#cite_note-382) As of September 2025, she was the second-most followed woman on the site, with over 104 million Twitter followers, and the sixth most followed musician across social media with a combined total of over 377 million followers across [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), Instagram, and Twitter.[[383]](#cite_note-383) In June 2017, *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine listed Perry among its "25 Most Influential People on the Internet" of the year, writing that her live-stream for *Witness* was "blazing a trail" for being "the closest any major entertainer has come to giving fans the kind of 'real' intimacy that social media purports to provide".[[384]](#cite_note-384)

She has been described as a [sex symbol](/wiki/Sex_symbol "Sex symbol"); *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* labeled her a "full-on male fantasy",[[9]](#cite_note-GQ-9) while *[Elle](/wiki/Elle_(magazine) "Elle (magazine)")* wrote her body looked "as though sketched by a teenage boy".[[22]](#cite_note-Elle-22) *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_(magazine) "Vice (magazine)")* called her a "'serious' popstar/woman/sex symbol".[[385]](#cite_note-385) Perry was placed at number one on the *[Maxim](/wiki/Maxim_(magazine) "Maxim (magazine)")* Hot 100 in 2010 as the "most beautiful woman in the world", with editor Joe Levy describing her as a "triple – no quadruple – kind of hot".[[386]](#cite_note-MaximHot100-386) *[Men's Health](/wiki/Men%27s_Health_(magazine) "Men's Health (magazine)")* readers voted her the "sexiest woman of 2013".[[387]](#cite_note-The_Hottest_Women_of_2013_-_Men's_Health-387) During November 2010, she mentioned being proud of and satisfied with her figure.[[388]](#cite_note-HarpersBazaar-388)

Perry's fashion often incorporates humor, bright colors, and food-related themes[[389]](#cite_note-389) such as her characteristic spinning peppermint swirl dress.[[390]](#cite_note-pb5-390) *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")* described her as "never exactly one to shy away from the outrageous or the extreme in any realm", and called her the "Queen of [Camp](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)")",[[391]](#cite_note-391)[[392]](#cite_note-Vogue2022-392) while *[Glamour](/wiki/Glamour_(magazine) "Glamour (magazine)")* named her the "queen of quirk".[[393]](#cite_note-393) In February 2009, Perry told *[Seventeen](/wiki/Seventeen_(American_magazine) "Seventeen (American magazine)")* that her fashion style was "a bit of a concoction of different things" and stated she enjoyed humor in her clothing.[[394]](#cite_note-17Style-394) She has also described herself as having "[multipersonality disorder](/wiki/Dissociative_identity_disorder "Dissociative identity disorder")" for fashion.[[388]](#cite_note-HarpersBazaar-388) Perry lists Gwen Stefani, [Shirley Manson](/wiki/Shirley_Manson "Shirley Manson"), [Chloë Sevigny](/wiki/Chlo%C3%AB_Sevigny "Chloë Sevigny"), [Daphne Guinness](/wiki/Daphne_Guinness "Daphne Guinness"), [Natalie Portman](/wiki/Natalie_Portman "Natalie Portman"), and the fictional character [Lolita](/wiki/Lolita "Lolita") as her style icons.[[34]](#cite_note-WMag-34)[[395]](#cite_note-395) In 2022, *Elle* dubbed her as "the [kitsch](/wiki/Kitsch "Kitsch")-loving pop star renowned for her uniquely experimental style", while *Vogue* described her style as "synonymous with outrageous, eye-catching ensembles that lean towards the theatrical".[[396]](#cite_note-396)[[397]](#cite_note-:1-397)

During the 2017 launch of her shoe collection, Katy Perry Collections, she said about shoes: "When I first got to L.A., I cultivated my style on a budget, always shopping at thrift stores or vintage stores. ... Once, I found these flats that looked like [Dalmatian dogs](/wiki/Dalmatian_(dog) "Dalmatian (dog)"). They had ears that moved and a tongue that stuck out. They were such a conversation piece. That's what is so great about fashion. ... It's a form of communication. You don't have to start a smoking habit to start talking to someone. You can just wear cool shoes. It's an icebreaker."[[398]](#cite_note-398)

### Sexual misconduct allegations

In 2018, while serving as a judge on *American Idol*, Perry – then aged 33 – gave an unwanted kiss to a 19-year-old contestant, Benjamin Glaze. When Glaze admitted that he had never kissed a girl (a reference to Perry's song "I Kissed a Girl"), she gestured for him to come up to her for a kiss on the cheek but then kissed him on the lips. Her behavior was met with backlash, with some pointing out that if she had been a male judge who kissed a woman the same way, the reaction would have been different. Glaze later admitted that the experience made him feel uncomfortable, as it was his first kiss and he wanted it to be special, although he rejected the notion that he felt sexually harassed by Perry.[[399]](#cite_note-399)

In August 2019, Josh Kloss, Perry's co-star in the "Teenage Dream" music video, accused her of sexual misconduct.[[400]](#cite_note-400) In an [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") post, Kloss alleged that, during a party at a skating rink, Perry pulled on his sweatpants and underwear, exposing his penis to her male friends. He also said her management prevented him from speaking about his time with the singer. Johnny Wujek, the creative director of said party defended Perry, saying that she "would never do something like that" and accused Kloss of having an "ongoing obsession" with her.[[401]](#cite_note-401) After initially refraining from responding to this, believing it would have detracted from the [MeToo movement](/wiki/MeToo_movement "MeToo movement"), Perry has also denied Kloss' claims.[[402]](#cite_note-402)

## Legacy

Several media outlets such as *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and *[Glamour](/wiki/Glamour_(magazine) "Glamour (magazine)")* have referred to her as the "[Queen of Pop](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music")", while others like *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")*, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*, and *[InStyle](/wiki/InStyle "InStyle")* have dubbed Perry the "Queen of Camp".[[408]](#cite_note-408) Andrew Unterberger of *Billboard* described *Teenage Dream* "one of the defining LPs from a new golden age in mega-pop"[[409]](#cite_note-409) while Christopher Rosa of *Glamour* named her as an influence to the [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") sound and style of the 2010s, adding that her singles are "some of the most recognizable, iconic, and impactful hits in pop history."[[410]](#cite_note-GlamourBestWorst-410) Perry was named "one of the last decade's most reliable and successful hitmakers" by the [Official Charts Company](/wiki/Official_Charts_Company "Official Charts Company") in 2022.[[411]](#cite_note-411)

Additionally, Perry was included in *Glamour*'s "104 Women Who Defined the Decade in Pop Culture" list of the 2010s. It stated Perry "did more than just break chart records. She was one of the driving forces behind the sound of pop radio in the 2010s" for her tracks that were "glossy, booming, sugary-sweet, and undeniably catchy".[[410]](#cite_note-GlamourBestWorst-410)[[412]](#cite_note-412) [*Variety*](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)") included Perry in their *Variety* 500 list of the most influential business leaders, calling her a "global phenomenon" and a "dedicated artist and tireless self-promoter who has leveraged chart-topping hits, sold-out stadium shows, and staggering endorsement deals to become one of the richest and most influential pop stars alive."[[413]](#cite_note-413) A 2017 journal published by *[Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts](/wiki/Psychology_of_Aesthetics,_Creativity,_and_the_Arts "Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts")* studying structural patterns in the melodies of [earworm](/wiki/Earworm "Earworm") songs compiled lists of [catchiest](/wiki/Catchiness "Catchiness") tracks from 3,000 participants, in which Perry's "California Gurls" ranked number six.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJakubowskiFinkelStewartMüllensiefen2017122–135-414) She has been called a "[gay icon](/wiki/Gay_icon "Gay icon")" by Taylor Henderson of *[Out](/wiki/Out_(magazine) "Out (magazine)")*, noting how "I Kissed a Girl" helped fans explore their sexuality and how Perry openly embraced the [LGBTQ+ community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community").[[280]](#cite_note-Out2021-280)

Perry's music has been described by *Out* as having a "lasting legacy", with American singers [Fletcher](/wiki/Fletcher_(singer) "Fletcher (singer)") sampling "I Kissed a Girl" and [Olivia Rodrigo](/wiki/Olivia_Rodrigo "Olivia Rodrigo") referencing *Teenage Dream* on "[Brutal](/wiki/Brutal_(song) "Brutal (song)")".[[280]](#cite_note-Out2021-280) Additionally, other artists such as [Halsey](/wiki/Halsey_(singer) "Halsey (singer)") and [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") have praised Perry's work, with the former calling *Teenage Dream* the "perfect pop album" and Grande saying "The One That Got Away" is "one of the biggest and most perfectly written pop songs ever from one of the best pop albums of all time."[[415]](#cite_note-415)[[416]](#cite_note-:2-416)

## Other ventures

### Philanthropy

[![Perry posing for photographers at a UNICEF gala](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Katy_Perry_UNICEF_2012.jpg/250px-Katy_Perry_UNICEF_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katy_Perry_UNICEF_2012.jpg)

Perry became a UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador in December 2013.

Perry has supported various charitable organizations and causes throughout her career. She has contributed to organizations aimed at improving the lives and welfare of children in particular. In April 2013, she joined [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") to assist children in Madagascar with education and nutrition.[[417]](#cite_note-417) On December 3, 2013, she was officially named a [UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador](/wiki/List_of_UNICEF_Goodwill_Ambassadors "List of UNICEF Goodwill Ambassadors"), "with a special focus on engaging young people in the agency's work to improve the lives of the world's most vulnerable children and adolescents."[[418]](#cite_note-UNICEFGoodwillAmbassador-418) She arranged for a portion of the money generated from tickets to her Prismatic World Tour to go to UNICEF.[[419]](#cite_note-PrismaticNorthAmerica-419) In September 2010, she helped build and design the Boys Hope/Girls Hope foundation shelter for youth in Baltimore, Maryland along with [Raven-Symoné](/wiki/Raven-Symon%C3%A9 "Raven-Symoné"), [Shaquille O'Neal](/wiki/Shaquille_O%27Neal "Shaquille O'Neal"), and the cast of *[Extreme Makeover: Home Edition](/wiki/Extreme_Makeover:_Home_Edition "Extreme Makeover: Home Edition")*.[[420]](#cite_note-420) In 2010, Perry and [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj") performed a cover of "[Girls Just Want to Have Fun](/wiki/Girls_Just_Want_to_Have_Fun "Girls Just Want to Have Fun")" for service members during the 2010 [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") Divas Salute the Troops concert.[[421]](#cite_note-421)

She has also supported children's education and well-being. All profits from sales of the album *The Gay Nineties Old Tyme Music: Daisy Bell*, which includes her rendition of "[Daisy Bell (Bicycle Built for Two)](/wiki/Daisy_Bell "Daisy Bell")", were donated to the charity [Little Kids Rock](/wiki/Little_Kids_Rock "Little Kids Rock"), which supports musical education in underprivileged elementary schools.[[201]](#cite_note-MarkRyder-201) In June 2014, she teamed up with [Staples Inc.](/wiki/Staples_Inc. "Staples Inc.") for a project entitled "Make Roar Happen" which donated $1 million to [DonorsChoose](/wiki/DonorsChoose "DonorsChoose"), an organization that supports teachers and funds classroom resources in public schools.[[422]](#cite_note-422) In May 2016, she worked with UNICEF to improve child care quality in Vietnam, hoping to "break the cycle of poverty and drastically improve children's health, education and well-being".[[423]](#cite_note-423) The following month, UNICEF announced that Perry would receive the [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn") Humanitarian Award "for her work as a UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador in support of the world's most vulnerable children" at their annual Snowflake Ball in November.[[424]](#cite_note-424) All Spotify streams of her 2021 cover of "All You Need Is Love" generated $1 in donations for the charity [Baby2Baby](/wiki/Baby2Baby "Baby2Baby").[[291]](#cite_note-AllYouNeedIsLove-291)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Katy_Perry_and_Nicki_Minaj_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Katy_Perry_and_Nicki_Minaj_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katy_Perry_and_Nicki_Minaj_(cropped).jpg)

Perry and [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj") (right) performing for service members during the 2010 VH1 Divas Salute the Troops concert

Perry has supported organizations aimed at aiding people suffering with diseases including cancer and [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS"). In 2008, she donated a [plaster cast](/wiki/Plaster_cast "Plaster cast") of her breasts that had been painted by her then-boyfriend Travie McCoy to raise money for the [Keep A Breast Foundation](/wiki/Keep_A_Breast_Foundation "Keep A Breast Foundation").[[425]](#cite_note-425)[[426]](#cite_note-426) She hosted and performed at the We Can Survive concert along with [Bonnie McKee](/wiki/Bonnie_McKee "Bonnie McKee"), [Kacey Musgraves](/wiki/Kacey_Musgraves "Kacey Musgraves"), [Sara Bareilles](/wiki/Sara_Bareilles "Sara Bareilles"), [Ellie Goulding](/wiki/Ellie_Goulding "Ellie Goulding"), and duo [Tegan and Sara](/wiki/Tegan_and_Sara "Tegan and Sara") at the Hollywood Bowl in Los Angeles on October 23, 2013. The concert's profits were donated to [Young Survival Coalition](/wiki/Young_Survival_Coalition "Young Survival Coalition"), an organization aiding breast cancer in young women.[[427]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Bowl-427) In June 2009, she designed an item of clothing for [H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M")'s "Fashion Against AIDS" campaign, which raises money for HIV/AIDS awareness projects.[[428]](#cite_note-428) On February 26, 2017, she served as a co-chair alongside various celebrities such as [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), [Lea Michele](/wiki/Lea_Michele "Lea Michele"), [Jim Carrey](/wiki/Jim_Carrey "Jim Carrey"), [Jared Leto](/wiki/Jared_Leto "Jared Leto"), and [Kevin Spacey](/wiki/Kevin_Spacey "Kevin Spacey") for the 25th Annual [Elton John AIDS Foundation Academy Award Party](/wiki/Elton_John_AIDS_Foundation_Academy_Award_Party "Elton John AIDS Foundation Academy Award Party"), a fundraiser for HIV/AIDS healthcare.[[429]](#cite_note-429)

The proceeds from Perry's single "Part of Me" were donated to the charity [MusiCares](/wiki/MusiCares "MusiCares"), which helps musicians in times of need.[[430]](#cite_note-430) During her California Dreams Tour, she raised over $175,000 for the Tickets-For-Charity fundraiser. The money was divided between three charities: the [Children's Health Fund](/wiki/Children%27s_Health_Fund "Children's Health Fund") (CHF), Generosity Water, and [The Humane Society of the United States](/wiki/The_Humane_Society_of_the_United_States "The Humane Society of the United States").[[431]](#cite_note-431) On her 27th birthday, Perry set up a donations page for the [Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals](/wiki/Society_for_the_Prevention_of_Cruelty_to_Animals "Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals") Auckland,[[432]](#cite_note-432) and set up a similar page benefiting the [David Lynch Foundation](/wiki/David_Lynch_Foundation "David Lynch Foundation") for her 28th birthday.[[433]](#cite_note-433) On March 29, 2014, she helped raise $2.4 million for the [Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art,_Los_Angeles "Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles") in Los Angeles along with other celebrities such as [Ryan Seacrest](/wiki/Ryan_Seacrest "Ryan Seacrest"), [Pharrell Williams](/wiki/Pharrell_Williams "Pharrell Williams"), [Tim Allen](/wiki/Tim_Allen "Tim Allen"), [Lisa Edelstein](/wiki/Lisa_Edelstein "Lisa Edelstein"), and [Riley Keough](/wiki/Riley_Keough "Riley Keough").[[434]](#cite_note-434)

Perry performed at the [One Love Manchester](/wiki/One_Love_Manchester "One Love Manchester") benefit concert for the victims of the 2017 [Manchester Arena bombing](/wiki/Manchester_Arena_bombing "Manchester Arena bombing"), among various performers including its organizer [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande"), which was broadcast live on June 4, 2017, on radio and television stations around the world.[[435]](#cite_note-435) In March 2018, Perry announced *Witness: Coming Home*, a benefit concert that was held in her hometown of Santa Barbara on May 19, 2018. The concert benefited those recovering from the aftermath of the [2017 California wildfires](/wiki/2017_California_wildfires "2017 California wildfires") and [2018 Southern California mudflows](/wiki/2018_Southern_California_mudflows "2018 Southern California mudflows"). Perry partnered with the Santa Barbara Foundation, the 93108 Fund and The 805 UndocuFund, organizations which help in assisting members of the community in the Santa Barbara area through grants and various philanthropic efforts.[[436]](#cite_note-WitnessComingHome-436)

### Activism and political views

[![Hillary Clinton and Perry pose with hands connected at a fundraising concert](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Katy_Perry_Hillary_Clinton%2C_I%27m_With_Her_Concert.jpg/250px-Katy_Perry_Hillary_Clinton%2C_I%27m_With_Her_Concert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katy_Perry_Hillary_Clinton,_I%27m_With_Her_Concert.jpg)

Perry performed at multiple ceremonies for Democratic presidential candidate [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") during her [2016 campaign](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_2016_presidential_campaign "Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign").

Perry has publicly advocated for [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States") and admitted that she wrote "I Kissed a Girl" about her own [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual") experiences with other women. In 2017, she received a Nation Equality Award from [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign") for "using her powerful voice and international platform to speak out for LGBTQ equality".[[437]](#cite_note-437) During her acceptance speech, she discussed having bisexual experiences, her fluid sexuality, and thanked the [LGBTQ+ community](/wiki/LGBTQ%2B_community "LGBTQ+ community").[[438]](#cite_note-438) In an *Out* interview in 2021, she was heralded as a "[gay icon](/wiki/Gay_icon "Gay icon")" with "I Kissed a Girl" being called a "bonafide queer anthem". Perry continued to thank the LGBTQ+ community in the same interview, saying: "I came from a very sheltered upbringing where it wasn't okay to be friends with anyone from that community. And now that is my community." She also mentioned "I wouldn't have survived without the community and it's amazing how full circle it's come and how much growth has happened since I started."[[280]](#cite_note-Out2021-280) Tomás Mier of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* remarked Perry "championed queer folks, especially [drag queens](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen"), throughout her career".[[404]](#cite_note-:3-404)

She dedicated the music video to her song "Firework" to the [It Gets Better Project](/wiki/It_Gets_Better_Project "It Gets Better Project").[[439]](#cite_note-439) In June 2012, Perry expressed her hopes for LGBT equality, commenting "hopefully, we will look back at this moment and think like we do now concerning [other] [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights") issues. We'll just shake our heads in disbelief, saying, 'Thank God we've evolved.' That would be my prayer for the future."[[440]](#cite_note-440) In December 2012, Perry was awarded the Trevor Hero Award by [The Trevor Project](/wiki/The_Trevor_Project "The Trevor Project") for her work and activism on behalf of LGBT youth.[[441]](#cite_note-TrevorHero-441)

Perry identifies as a [feminist](/wiki/Feminist "Feminist"),[[442]](#cite_note-442) and appeared in April 2013 in a video clip for the "Chime for Change" campaign that aimed to spread female empowerment.[[443]](#cite_note-443) She has also said that America's lack of free [health care](/wiki/Health_care_in_the_United_States "Health care in the United States") drove her "absolutely crazy".[[444]](#cite_note-444) Following the [shooting at the Pulse nightclub in Orlando](/wiki/Orlando_nightclub_shooting "Orlando nightclub shooting") in June 2016, Perry and nearly 200 other artists and executives in music signed an open letter organized by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* addressed to [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") demanding increased [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control") in the United States.[[445]](#cite_note-445)

Through Twitter and by performing at rallies, Perry supported President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in his run for re-election and praised his support for [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States")[[446]](#cite_note-bamanails-446) and LGBT equality.[[447]](#cite_note-447) She performed at three rallies for Obama, in Los Angeles, Las Vegas, and Wisconsin, singing a rendition of "[Let's Stay Together](/wiki/Let%27s_Stay_Together_(Al_Green_song) "Let's Stay Together (Al Green song)")" as well as a number of her songs. During her Las Vegas performance she wore a dress made to replicate a voting ballot, with Obama's box filled in.[[448]](#cite_note-448) On Twitter, she encouraged her followers to vote for Obama.[[449]](#cite_note-449)

In August 2013, prior to a performance in Australia, Perry stated that she did not agree with conservative Australian opposition leader [Tony Abbott](/wiki/Tony_Abbott "Tony Abbott")'s stance against same-sex marriage, telling him "I love you as a human being but I can't give you my vote". Abbott had dialed in to a talkback segment on Sydney radio station [2Day FM](/wiki/2Day_FM "2Day FM") to ask Perry when she would return to Australia for a full tour.[[450]](#cite_note-TonyAbbott-450) In April 2014, she publicly supported [Marianne Williamson](/wiki/Marianne_Williamson "Marianne Williamson") in her campaign for [California's 33rd congressional district](/wiki/California%27s_33rd_congressional_district "California's 33rd congressional district") by attending a political press event.[[451]](#cite_note-451) She endorsed [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") in the [United States Senate election in California](/wiki/United_States_Senate_election_in_California,_2016 "United States Senate election in California, 2016"), and organized a fundraiser for Harris at her home in Los Angeles in November 2016.[[452]](#cite_note-452) Perry also publicly endorsed former Secretary of State [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") for president in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election").[[453]](#cite_note-453)[[454]](#cite_note-454) She performed alongside Elton John at a fundraising concert for Clinton in New York City in March 2016.[[455]](#cite_note-455) Perry also spoke and performed at the [2016 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2016_Democratic_National_Convention "2016 Democratic National Convention") in support of Clinton.[[456]](#cite_note-456)

Four years later, she supported [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") and Kamala Harris during the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), praising the latter as a leader who had "experience we desperately need right now" and believed that the former "choosing her as his running mate is already a testament to his decision making".[[457]](#cite_note-457) In 2022, Perry posted a picture of herself voting for conservative candidate [Rick Caruso](/wiki/Rick_Caruso "Rick Caruso") in the [2022 Los Angeles mayoral election](/wiki/2022_Los_Angeles_mayoral_election "2022 Los Angeles mayoral election"), which Democrats heavily criticized her for.[[458]](#cite_note-458) During the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), she endorsed Harris for office, stating "I've always known her to fight for the most vulnerable, to speak up for the voiceless, and to protect our rights as women to make decisions about our own bodies".[[459]](#cite_note-459)

### Attempted purchase of Los Feliz property

In 2015, Perry attempted to purchase a former convent in Los Angeles, which sparked a years-long legal battle involving the Archdiocese of Los Angeles, members of the Sisters of the Most Holy and Immaculate Heart of Mary, and real estate developer Dana Hollister. Initially, she offered the archdiocese $14.5 million in cash to buy the eight-acre Los Feliz property.[[460]](#cite_note-460) However, two elderly nuns, Sister Rita Callanan and Sister Catherine Rose Holzman, opposed the sale and attempted to sell the property to Hollister instead at $15.5 million, believing she would preserve the site and open it to the public.[[461]](#cite_note-461) The archdiocese sued, asserting the nuns lacked authority to sell the property and that any sale over $7.5 million required Vatican approval.[[462]](#cite_note-462)

In 2016, a Los Angeles Superior Court judge ruled the sale to Hollister invalid due to the absence of necessary church approval, clearing the way for Perry's purchase.[[463]](#cite_note-463) In 2017, a jury determined Hollister had intentionally interfered with Perry's real estate deal and awarded $5 million in compensatory damages—$3.47 million to the archdiocese and $1.57 million to Perry's company.[[464]](#cite_note-464) The judge later imposed an additional $10 million in punitive damages, divided between Perry and the archdiocese.[[465]](#cite_note-465) Holzman died in court in 2018.[[466]](#cite_note-v201-466)

## Achievements

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Katy Perry](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Katy_Perry "List of awards and nominations received by Katy Perry") and [List of Katy Perry records and achievements](/wiki/List_of_Katy_Perry_records_and_achievements "List of Katy Perry records and achievements")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Katy_Perry_%2815262665623%29.jpg/250px-Katy_Perry_%2815262665623%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katy_Perry_(15262665623).jpg)

Perry at the [ARIA Music Awards](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards") in November 2014

Throughout her career, Perry has won five [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards"),[[467]](#cite_note-467) 14 [People's Choice Awards](/wiki/People%27s_Choice_Awards "People's Choice Awards"),[[468]](#cite_note-468) 20 [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records"),[[100]](#cite_note-Guinness2010-100)[[123]](#cite_note-Guinness2013-123)[[197]](#cite_note-Guinness2016-197)[[380]](#cite_note-Guinness2015-380)[[469]](#cite_note-469) a [Brit Award](/wiki/Brit_Award "Brit Award"),[[470]](#cite_note-Brit2009-470) and a [Juno Award](/wiki/Juno_Award "Juno Award").[[115]](#cite_note-Juno2011-115) In September 2012, *Billboard* dubbed her the "Woman of the Year".[[160]](#cite_note-WomanOfTheYear-160) From May 2010 to September 2011, the singer spent a record-breaking 69 consecutive weeks in the top ten of the *Billboard* Hot 100.[[124]](#cite_note-adeles-someone-124)[[471]](#cite_note-billboardhot100perrycelebrates-471) *Teenage Dream* became the first album by a female artist to produce five number-one *Billboard* Hot 100 singles, and the second album overall after Michael Jackson's *Bad*.[[121]](#cite_note-TieMichael-121) In the United States, she has accumulated nine number-one singles on the *Billboard* Hot 100, her most recent being "Dark Horse",[[184]](#cite_note-NumberOneHorse-184) and holds the record for having 18 consecutive number-one songs on the *Billboard* [Dance Club Songs](/wiki/Dance_Club_Songs "Dance Club Songs") chart.[[472]](#cite_note-472)

*According to Billboard*, she is the 15th most successful dance club artist of all time.[[473]](#cite_note-473) The magazine additionally ranked her fourth on its "Greatest of All Time Pop Songs Artists" list,[[474]](#cite_note-474) fifth on the 2025 "Top 100 Women Artists of the 21st Century" list,[[475]](#cite_note-475) and 25th on the 2024 "[Greatest Pop Stars of the 21st Century](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Greatest_Pop_Stars#21st_century "Billboard's Greatest Pop Stars")" list,[[476]](#cite_note-476) included *Teenage Dream* and *Prism* among its "Greatest of All Time *Billboard 200* Albums by Women" list,[[477]](#cite_note-477) and ranked "Dark Horse" at number 100 on its "Greatest of All Time Hot 100 Songs"[[478]](#cite_note-478) as well as one of its "Greatest of All Time Hot 100 Songs by Women" along with "E.T.", "Firework", and "California Gurls".[[479]](#cite_note-479) In June 2015, her music video for "Dark Horse" became the first video by a female artist to reach one billion views on Vevo.[[480]](#cite_note-480)[[481]](#cite_note-481) The following month, her music video for "Roar" reached one billion views on Vevo, making her the first artist to have multiple videos with one billion views.[[482]](#cite_note-482) During May 2024, it reached four billion views, making it the most viewed music video by a female artist on YouTube at that time.[[483]](#cite_note-483)

With more than 151 million records sold worldwide,[[484]](#cite_note-484) Perry is [one of the best-selling music artists of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").[[485]](#cite_note-485) She is 10th among female artists who have sold the most records worldwide and the best-selling female artist of Capitol Records.[[486]](#cite_note-486)[[487]](#cite_note-487)[[488]](#cite_note-488) She was declared the Top Global Female Recording Artist of 2013 by the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI).[[198]](#cite_note-IFPI-198) According to RIAA, Perry is the thirteenth [top digital singles artist](/wiki/List_of_highest-certified_music_artists_in_the_United_States "List of highest-certified music artists in the United States") in the United States, with 121.5 million certified song units in the country including on-demand streams[[489]](#cite_note-489) and also has 19 million certified album units, totaling 140.5 million certified units in the United States.[[490]](#cite_note-490) In 2017, she became the first artist to have three Diamond-certified songs from the RIAA. Her new certification for "Roar" joined her previously Diamond-certified songs "Dark Horse" and "Firework".[[491]](#cite_note-491) Seven years later, "California Gurls", "Teenage Dream", "E.T.", and *Teenage Dream* have also been certified Diamond, bringing a total of seven Diamond certifications from the organization.[[492]](#cite_note-492) The latter is the first and only album with four Diamond-certified songs on its tracklist.[[493]](#cite_note-493) In addition, Perry is the only artist to have three songs selling six million digital downloads, with "Firework" selling 7 million, "Roar" at 6.11 million, and "Dark Horse" at 6.08 million according to Nielsen SoundScan.[[494]](#cite_note-494) Elsewhere, "Roar" has become Australia's fourth most certified single of all time (22-times Platinum) according to the [Australian Recording Industry Association](/wiki/Australian_Recording_Industry_Association "Australian Recording Industry Association") (ARIA). As of 2025, Perry has six more singles that are certified 10-times Platinum or higher by the ARIA.[[495]](#cite_note-495) In Canada, *Teenage Dream* was the eighth best-selling album of the 2010s,[[496]](#cite_note-496) and it was certified Diamond by [Music Canada](/wiki/Music_Canada "Music Canada"), along with seven of her singles.[[497]](#cite_note-497) In Brazil, Perry has 15 songs certified Diamond or higher by [Pro-Música Brasil](/wiki/Pro-M%C3%BAsica_Brasil "Pro-Música Brasil") (PMB),[[498]](#cite_note-498) and in Italy, the [Federazione Industria Musicale Italiana](/wiki/Federazione_Industria_Musicale_Italiana "Federazione Industria Musicale Italiana") (FIMI) listed her as the 98th most certified music artist since the certification database became available in 2009.[[499]](#cite_note-499) As of November 2025, Perry has garnered over 125 billion cumulative streams from various digital and streaming platforms.[[500]](#cite_note-500)

In 2011, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* ranked her third on their "Top-Earning Women In Music" list with earnings of $44 million[[501]](#cite_note-Forbes2011-501) and fifth on their 2012 list with $45 million.[[502]](#cite_note-Forbes2012-502) She subsequently ranked seventh on the 2013 *Forbes* list for "Top-Earning Women In Music" with $39 million earned,[[503]](#cite_note-503) and fifth on their 2014 list with $40 million.[[504]](#cite_note-504) With earnings of $135 million, *Forbes* also ranked Perry number one on their 2015 "Top-Earning Women In Music" list as well as the "World's Highest-Paid Musicians" and declared her the highest earning female celebrity in 2015, placing her at number 3 on the *Forbes* Celebrity 100 list.[[505]](#cite_note-505) In 2016, the magazine estimated her net worth was $125 million,[[506]](#cite_note-506) and ranked her number six on their list of "Highest-Paid Women in Music" with earnings of $41 million.[[507]](#cite_note-507) The following year, she was ranked number nine on the list with $33 million.[[508]](#cite_note-508) In 2018, she topped its "Highest-Paid Women in Music" listing and ranked at number four on the "Highest-Paid Female Celebrities" list, with earnings of $83 million.[[509]](#cite_note-509)[[510]](#cite_note-510) Perry subsequently was placed at number four on the 2019 "Highest-Paid Women in Music" listings, with $57.5 million.[[511]](#cite_note-511) Later that year, with earnings of $530 million throughout the 2010s, the magazine also ranked her as the ninth-highest-earning musician of the decade.[[512]](#cite_note-512) Perry is among the [wealthiest musical artists](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_by_net_worth "List of music artists by net worth"); during September 2023, *Forbes* surmised her net worth was $340 million.[[513]](#cite_note-513) This increased to $360 million by June 2025, where she was included on the magazine's 2025 list of "America's Richest Women Celebrities".[[514]](#cite_note-514)
